licenses.name: GNU General Public License v2.0 only
licenses.description: Strong copyleft license that requires derivatives to be GPL-2.0; used by the Linux kernel.

metadata.explanation_hint: Historically significant (Linux kernel). Incompatible with Apache-2.0 and GPL-3.0 unless 'or later' clause is present. Use GPL-3.0 for new projects.

licenses.name: GNU General Public License v3.0 only
licenses.description: Ensures all derivatives and distributed versions remain open source.

metadata.explanation_hint: Use when you want to guarantee all downstream modifications stay open.

licenses.name: GNU Lesser General Public License v2.1 only
licenses.description: Allows proprietary software to link against the library without copyleft infection, but modifications to the library itself must be shared.

metadata.explanation_hint: Key nuance: dynamic linking is generally safe for proprietary apps; static linking may trigger copyleft depending on interpretation. Always clarify linking type.

licenses.name: GNU Lesser General Public License v3.0 only
licenses.description: LGPL-2.1 terms plus GPL-3.0 additions (anti-tivoization, patent terms). Preferred for new libraries.

metadata.explanation_hint: Superset of LGPL-2.1 with GPL-3.0 protections. Use for libraries where you want wide adoption but still require library modifications to be shared.

licenses.name: GNU Affero General Public License v3.0
licenses.description: GPL-3.0 extended with a network-use (SaaS) copyleft clause: users who interact over a network must be given source access.

metadata.explanation_hint: The 'viral SaaS' license. If you run AGPL software as a web service, you must offer users the source. Ideal for open-core business models. Businesses often avoid it for internal tools.
